corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Chiefly in Latin, English, French, Spanish, Dutch, German, and Italian. Also includes Aabic, Persian, Slavic, Hebrew, Turkish, Syriac, and Algonquian.

did.abstract: The collection is comprised of manuscript volumes, manuscript and incunabula leaves and fragments, and bound and unbound partial volumes of manuscripts and printes books, dating from approximately 850-1955, collected by educator, collector, and dealer Otto F. Ege. The collection consists of religious texts, such as bibles, books of hours, prayer books, hymnals, missals, psalters, choir books, and well as histories and literature from Europe, Persia, and Asia. The collection also contains a small amount of material documenting the life and career of Otto F. Ege and to a lesser extent Louise Ege, including writings, correspondence, photographs, newspaper clippings, and catalogs or advertisements.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Open to research.
Some items in Series I - III and most of series IV are temporarily inaccessible pending preservation actions.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into five series: Series I. Manuscript Volumes, circa 1150-circa 1826. Series II. Portfolios, 1100-1935. Series. III. Manuscript and Manuscript Volume Fragments, circa 850-circa 1900. Series IV. Printed Material, circa 1472-1955. Series V. Personal Papers, circa 1890-circa 1961.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Otto F. Ege (1888-1951) was an educator, collector of rare books and manuscripts and, most notably, a dealer, known for removing leaves from manuscript and print volumes for exhibition and sale. In the early 1920s he began to disassemble incomplete medieval manuscripts and incunabula and to sell the leaves, at first as single items but soon in a series of boxed portfolios intended to illustrate cultural subjects, including the history of the Bible and "Fifty Original Leaves from Medieval Manuscripts." Ege defended his book breaking on educational grounds, even authoring an article in 1938 titled "I am a Biblioclast."

Otto F. Ege was born in Reading, Pennsylvania, on April 14, 1888, to John Ege and Pauline Ege (nee Wonnberger). He graduated from Reading High School in 1907 and studied design and art education at the Philadelphia Museum School of Art and New York University. In 1920 he moved to Cleveland, Ohio, and began a long career at the Cleveland Institute of Art, holding several positions including instructor, department chair, and dean (1946-1951). He also taught at Western Reserve University (Case Western) and lectured across Ohio, New York, and Pennsylvania. His teaching included the use of book art specimens (or fragments). Ege was an advocate for the teaching and appreciation of Book Arts. He became a noted expert on medieval manuscripts and early modern printed books. He served as tour guide and buyer for several libraries and museums in Ohio. Ege became a renowned collector and dealer.

Scott Gwara, in his extensive study of Ege, characterizes Ege's collecting in three phases. Between 1911 and 1934, Ege collected broadly, building his personal and teaching collection. During this time, Ege sold manuscript fragments taken from damaged or incomplete volumes. Between circa 1935 and circa 1946, Ege established a rare manuscripts and books trade business and sold fragments (from incomplete and intact volumes) in presentation mats and portfolios to museums, libraries, and educational organizations as well as middle-income collectors. The portfolios were compiled to illustrate topics in book history, such as famous bibles, medieval manuscripts and history of type. From about 1947 until his death on June 17, 1951, Ege focused his collecting and business on compiling and selling Original Leaves from Famous Books, exceptional leaves matted and encased as a portfolio set. Ege died before the project was complete, and the compilation and sell of the portfolio was completed by Louise H. Ege (nee Lange), his widow.

Ege's legacy as educator, collector, and dealer is complex. He is recognized as a strong advocate for the education in and appreciation of book arts and for creating the market for affordable manuscript and incunabula fragments. He was also criticized for his practice of breaking or disassembling and dispersing (by sell) unique and rare volumes. Proclaiming himself a biblioclast in a 1938 article in Avocations he defended his book-breaking practices for allowing more people the opportunity to see, own, learn about, and appreciate the art in manuscripts, incunabula, and fine books. Many single leaves and fragments collected and sold by Ege are held in North American libraries, their study in digital form has been foundational to the development of digital fragmentology.

Otto F. Ege died on June 17, 1951. He was survived by his wife Lousie H. Ege (nee Lange) and their daughter Elizabeth (Betty). Louise and Betty stewarded Ege's collection, sold or donated parts to various institutions. Ege's grandchildren sold the remainder of the collection to Yale University in 2015.
Porcheddu, Fred. 2007. "Otto F. Ege: Teacher, Collector, and Biblioclast." Art Documentation 26 (1): 4-14.
Gwara, Scott. Otto Ege's Manuscripts: A Study of Ege's Manuscript Collections, Portfolios, and Retail Trade. Cayce, SC: De Brailes Publishing, 2013.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Otto F. Ege Collection. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection is comprised of manuscript volumes, manuscript and incunabula leaves and fragments, and bound and unbound partial volumes of manuscripts and print volumes, dating from approximately 850-1955, collected by educator, collector, and dealer, Otto F. Ege. The collection consists of religious texts, such as bibles, books of hours, prayer books, hymnals, missals, psalters, choir books, and well as histories and literature from Europe, Persia, and Asia.

The collection also contains a small amount of material documenting the life and career of Otto F. Ege and to a lesser extent, Louise Ege, including writings, correspondence, photographs, newspaper clippings, and catalogs or advertisements. The collections does not include records documenting the financial side of Ege's career.

The Ege collection serves to illustrate a thousand years of book history, especially script and illumination, as well as components of the production (such as bindings) and trade of manuscripts and early books.
When known Ege titles and identifier and Cleveland Museum of Art and Gwara Handlist identifiers are included in the description.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Description of materials in the Otto F. Ege collection was repurposed from description provided by the sellers and donors and notes shared by Scott Gwara. Additional description and information of items in the collection were provided by Hannelore Segers, Agnieszka Rec, and Lisa Fagan Davis. This finding aid will be ammended as revisions to description are made by staff.

Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Beinecke Archival Description Processing Manual.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from John M. Freudenheim, Tom Freudenheim, Jo Freudenheim, Susan Core, Richard Core, and Michael Frisch, via JSJT Holdings, LLC, 2015-2018, with Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, Herman W. Liebert Book Fund, and the John W. Corwith Memorial Fund.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Except as noted below the material was maintained by Ege's heirs. Most of the manuscript volumes in Series I and several single manuscript leaves in Series III were previously on deposit with the Cleveland Museum of Art (CMA). These items are noted in the inventory with CMA numbers, for example "TR12333/2". A few items were previously on deposit with the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, before they were gifted to or purchased by the Beinecke in 2016.

separatedmaterial.head: Separated Materials
separatedmaterial.p: Empty portfolios for Original Leaves from Famous Books (numbered 72, 76, 82, 83, 84, 89, 102) and Original leaves from Famous Bibles (unnumbered), duplicate published writings, and copied newspaper clippings were discarded.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series consists of complete (or almost complete) manuscript volumes (circa 1150-circa 1826), primarily from Europe, that comprised Otto Ege's personal collection.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The volumes in the series are arranged chronologically.

did.physdesc: Layout: single column of 21 lines. Script: praegothica. Decoration: before f. 110: two-line initials in red or green ink with penwork highlights; after f. 110: two-line initials in red and blue ink. Binding: black sheep leather over paper pasteboards. Bottom board loose. Foliated 1-157 in modern ink including flyleaves. Folios 115r-118r, 119r, 120r are written in a second hand; perhaps a later amendment for missing text? Contemporary ownership inscription (f. 157v): Iste liber pertinet [illegible]. Possible shelfmark 381 on second front flyleaf in modern pencil. Formerly owned by Otto F. Ege (Ege 42.2000). Manuscript volume was on deposit with the Cleveland Museum of Art (CMA); inventory number on front flyleaf: TR 12828/50.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

Binding: green velvet binding over wood. Possible shelfmark 386 on second front flyleaf in modern pencil. Formerly owned by Otto F. Ege (Ege 44.2000). Manuscript volume was on deposit with the Cleveland Museum of Art (CMA); inventory number on front flyleaf: TR 12828/55.

did.physdesc: 1 item (140 leaves) : parchment ; 240 mm x 170 mm Layout: single column of 19 lines. Script: praegothica. Decoration: three-line initials in red ink. Rubrication throughout. Binding: vellum over wooden boards. Sparse marginal notes including musical notations. Formerly owned by Otto Ege (Ege 23.1999); his bookplate on end pastedown. Manuscript volume was on deposit with the Cleveland Museum of Art (CMA); inventory number on front flyleaf: TR 12333/5.

did.physdesc: 1 item (95 leaves) : parchment ; 270 mm x 205 mm Manuscript on parchment, in unidentified hand, containing a collection of Gospel lessons and saints' lives. Includes temporale (ff. 1r-72v), sanctorale (73r-92r), common of saints (92r-93r), dedication of the Church (93r-94r); in a second hand: lesson from the gospel of Mark (94r) and incipits of the four gospels (94v).Layout: single column of 26 lines. Decoration: eleven-line decorated initial (f.1r) in blue, red, and gold; multi-line initials in red and blue ink with contrasting penwork. Binding: blue leather over pasteboard. Modern, white label on spine with "30". Possible shelfmark 330 on front pastedown in modern pencil and on modern label. Formerly owned by Otto F. Ege (Ege 33.2000); his bookplate on front flyleaf. Manuscript volume was on deposit with the Cleveland Museum of Art (CMA); inventory number on front flyleaf: TR 12828/33.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Northern France, almost certainly diocese of Sens; 95 folios; Perhaps missing all the Easter lessons, possibly a whole quire, given the very short reading assigned for Easter Day, but this may have been in a separate volume. (Ege 33.2000; TR 12828/33; Not in Census)

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: Also described in the Yale online catalog.

did.physdesc: 1 item (7 + 78 + 7 leaves) : parchment ; 180 mm x 130 mm Layout: single column of 20 lines. Decoration: two ten-line decorated initials in blue, red, and gold; one six-line decorated initial in blue and red; three-line initials in red and blue ink throughout. Rubrication. Binding: 18th-century gold-tooled brown leather binding over pasteboard. Bookplate on front pastedown: "Alexander Moffatt / 23 Abercromby Place / Edingburgh". Formerly owned by Otto Ege (45.2000). Manuscript volume was on deposit with the Cleveland Museum of Art (CMA); inventory number on front flyleaf: TR 12828/58.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

Incomplete: several leaves missing at beginning (including calendar and litany) and end. Psalms begin in Ps. 9 and end in Ps. 103.8. (Ege 45.2000; TR 12828/58; Census II.1938, no. 11)

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: Also described in the Yale online catalog.

did.physdesc: 1 item (iii + 567 + ii leaves) : parchment ; 145 mm x 97 mm.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: England, probably Oxford; Manuscript on parchment, in unidentified hand, containing the Bible. Layout: double column of 48 lines. Decoration: historiated initials at Genesis, 1 Kings, 1 paralipomenon, Psalms, Parables, Isaiah, Jeremiah, Hosea, Matthew, and Romans. Decorated initials in red and blue ink with penwork. Rubrication. Binding: armorial style binding in black goat, gold-tooled. (Ege 343.1991; TR 12828/45; Not in Census)

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: Also described in the Yale online catalog.

did.physdesc: 1 volume (ii + 267 + ii leaves) : parchment and paper flyleaves ; 187 mm x 130 mm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: England or France; manuscript on parchment, in unidentified hand, containing the anonymous Summa virtutum. First folio is damaged with loss of text. Includes index on the first flyleaf. Contemporary manuscript annotations in margins throughout., illuminated initial at the beginning of the text, other blue and red initials, bound in later black velvet with morocco spine. (Ege 41.2000; TR 12828/49)

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: Also described in the Yale online catalog.

did.physdesc: 1 item (619 leaves) : parchment ; 258 mm x 177 mm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manuscript on parchment, in unidentified hand, containing the Bible. Incomplete: first folio and most of the interpretation of Hebrew names is wanting. Various corrections in margins throughout. Layout: double column of 46 lines. Decoration: multi-line puzzle initials in red and blue ink with penwork and extensions. Rubrication throughout. Binding: brown blind-tooled leather binding. Spine title in gold-tooling: Biblia sacra.Medieval pagination with several mistakes: e.g. skips from 470 to 480 without loss of text. (Ege 22.1999; TR 12333/4; not in Census)

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: Also described in the Yale online catalog.

did.physdesc: 1 item (616 leaves) : parchment ; 135 mm x 90 mm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: France, doubtless Paris; manuscript on parchment, in unidentified hand, containing the Bible. Incomplete: first folio missing. Layout: double column of 45 lines. Decoration: historiated initials throughout; two-line initials in alternating red and blue ink and rubrication throughout. Binding: red gold-tooled goat leather binding. Gilt edges. Spine title in gold tooling: Biblia manuscripta. (Ege 482.1984; TR12828/42; Not in Census)

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: Also described in the Yale online catalog.

did.unittitle: Psalter and Book of Hours (of Unidentified Use)
did.physdesc: 1 item (324 leaves) : parchment ; 17 mm x 82 mm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Northeastern France or Flanders (diocese of Cambrai?); Illuminated manuscript on parchment containing a calendar--perhaps originally from a different manuscript, a collection of psalms (psalter), and a collection of prayers making up the book of hours. The psalter contains Psalms 1-150 and includes many historiated initials (ff. 1r-207v). The book of hours includes: canticles (ff. 207v-224v), Athanasian creed (ff. 224v-228r), a collection of prayers for specific occasions (ff. 234v-238v), Hours of the virgin (ff. 239r-287r), Seasonal prayers and suffrages (ff. 278r-283r), long hours of the Holy Spirit (ff. 287r-300r), and long hours of the Cross (ff. 300v-317v); Layout: single column of 16 lines; Decoration: several multi-line historiated initials, multi-line decorative initials. Two-line initials in gold on pink and blue grounds with simple white penwork; alternating blue and gold versal initials; elaborate line-fillers of many designs; Binding: modern brown calf leather binding, silver clasp; Medieval foliation in pen in the upper margin of each recto, alternating red and black. This foliation ends at fol. 228; Use of the book of hours not specified. Perhaps for Tournai or Cambrai (St. Foyllon, bishop of Cambrai, in suffrages). (Ege 345.1991; TR 12828/27; Census II.1939, no. 17)

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: Also described in the Yale online catalog.

did.physdesc: 1 item (214 leaves) : parchment ; 155 mm x 93 mm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: England; Illuminated manuscript on parchment, in unidentified hand, containing the book of psalms. Also contains canticles (191v-207r), Athanasian creed (208r-210v), and a collection of various prayers (saints include SS. Eadmunde, Cuthbert, Dunstan and Robert). Closes with a number of medical recipes (214v) and a document dated to 1320; Layout: single column of 18 lines. Decoration: historiated initials throughout (1r, 31r, 50r, 67v, 68v, 87r, 110v, 130r, and 157v). Numerous two-line figural initials. Single-line versal initials in gold on red and blue ground. Decoration: historiated initials throughout (1r, 31r, 50r, 67v, 68v, 87r, 110v, 130r, and 157v). Numerous two-line figural initials. Single-line versal initials in gold on red and blue ground. Binding: Bound by F. Bedford (signed) in stamped morocco, on spine: PSALTERIUM / MS. ANGL. / CIRC. 1240-50." Stitched on four double-bands. Gilt edges. (Ege 8.1999; TR 12333/8; De Ricci, Census II.1939, no. 15)

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: Also described in the Yale online catalog.

did.physdesc: 1 item (176 leaves) : parchment ; 270 mm x 207 mm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Italy, probably Rome; Manuscript on parchment, in unidentified hand, containing the sanctorale section of a breviary. Includes the offices of numerous popes, some obscure: perhaps use of Rome. Layout: double column of 30 lines. Decoration: multi-line historiated initials, some on gold grounds; multi-line puzzle initials in blue and red with complex penwork; alternating red and blue multi-line initials with contrasting penwork. Rubrication throughout.Binding: modern blind-tooled half leather binding over wooden boards with modern clasps, straps wanting. Foliation in modern pencil. (Ege 349.1991; TR 12333/18; Not in Census)

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: Also described in the Yale online catalog.

did.physdesc: 1 item (100 leaves) : parchment ; 345 mm x 232 mm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manuscript on parchment, in unidentified hand, containing Latin translations of 20 Greek homilies, allegedly delivered by St. Peter and recorded by a certain Clemens. Also contains an incomplete table of contents for St. Fulgentius' De fide ad Petrum, once attributed to St. Augustine. Layout: single column of 39 lines.Decoration: multi-line initials in color on gold grounds with multi-colored swags and gold bezants; multi-line initials in blue ink with red penwork.Binding: gold-tooled three-quarter brown leather binding over paper covered pasteboard (Middle Hill boards). Spinal title: Missal. Spinal label: Clementis Epitome.(Ege 32.2000; TR 12828/29; Not in Census)

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: Also described in the Yale online catalog.

did.unittitle: Book of Hours, Use of Rouen
did.physdesc: 1 item (127 leaves) : parchment ; 173 mm x 125 mm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Normandy, diocese of Rouen, Illuminated manuscript, in unidentified hand, on parchment, of a book of hours, use of Rouen. Contains: calendar (ff. 1r-6v), complete hours of the virgin (ff. 7r-58v), hours of the cross (ff. 59r-66v), hours of the Holy Spirit (ff. 66v-69r), Gospel lessons (ff. 66r-74v), Penitential psalms (ff. 75r-87r), kyrie and other prayers (ff. 87r-92r), office of the dead (ff. 93v-123v), and O intemerata (ff. 123v-127r). Incomplete: six leaves from the calendar and one leaf at the beginning of the penitential psalms wanting. Layout: single column of 15 lines. Decoration: one half-page miniature, six large multi-line historiated initials, and multi-line decorative initials and flourished borders in gold. Rubrication throughout. Binding: red leather, gilt spine with spinal title: Heures Gotique.(Ege 9.1999; TR12333/9; Census II.1943, no. 41)

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: Also described in the Yale online catalog.

did.physdesc: 1 item (102 leaves) : parchment ; 412 mm x 290 mm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: England; Illuminated manuscript, in unidentified hand, on parchment, of a gradual, use of Sarum. Contains Temporale (ff. 1r-55v; including for the first Sunday of Advent through Epiphany, major feasts, and the remaining Sundays through the end of the year), texts for duplex feasts, Kyrie, Sanctus, Agnus Dei, Gloria (ff. 56r-62v), Common of Saints (ff. 63r-76v), and Sanctorale (ff. 77r-102v; including for St. Andrew, Bishop Wulstan, Bishop Richard, St. Edmund, John of Beverly, St. Richard, St. Dunstan, St. Aldhelm, etc.) Layout: single column of 15 four-line staves. Decoration: numerous large two-stave initials in gold on red and blue grounds with white penwork highlights and marginal sprays of ivy stems with green foliage and blue acanthus; one-staff blue initials with red work details; one-staff inked decorative initials of strapwork design, some with grotesque faces of beasts or humans (if human, usually bearded), frequent yellow wash; alternating red and blue versal initials. Rubricated throughout. Binding: 18th-century gold-tooled red leather binding, gilt; sewn on six raised bands. Colophon (f. 101v): Dum mundus stabit scriptor mulieres amabit. (Ege 1026.1988; TR 12828/34; Not in Census)

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: Also described in the Yale online catalog.

did.unittitle: Book of Hours, Use of Sarum
did.physdesc: 1 item (93 leaves) : parchment ; 193 mm x 125 mm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: England; Illuminated manuscript, in unidentified hand, on parchment, of a book of hourse, use of Sarum. Contains: calendar (ff. 1r-6v), Hours of the Virgin (ff. 7r-28v), Penitential psalms (ff. 29r-35r), Kyrie, Litany, petitions, prayers (ff. 35r-38v), Office of the dead (ff. 39r-63r), psalms and prayers (ff. 63r-77v), suffrages to St. Christopher and St. William of Norwich (ff. 77v-78v), prayers (ff. 78v-93v). Incomplete: missing opening of matins in hours of the virgin, opening of terce in hours of the virgin, opening of sext in hours of the virgin, opening of vespers in hours of the virgin, opening of compline in hours of the virgin, opening of penitential psalms, opening of the office of the dead. Layout: single column of 18 lines. Decoration: several multi-line, multi-colored initials on gold grounds with foliage swags in margin; two-line dentelle initials in gold on colored grounds with white penwork; alternating gold and blue versal initials with contrasting penwork; gold and blue line-fillers. Rubricated throughout. Some informal marginal drawings in brown ink (ff. 39r, 44v-45r, 54v-55r, 93v). Binding: gold-tooled black leather binding with gryphon holding a helm below the coronet of a baron on front cover, slightly scuffed; gold-tooled spine title "MISSALE." Gilt edges. Two foliations: 1-93 in modern pencil in the lefthand corner of every recto; 1-20 in modern pencil, every five folios thereafter with a few deviations, skips leaf between fols 50-55.(Ege 31.2000; TR 12828/25; Census II.1943-44, no. 46.)

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: Also described in the Yale online catalog.

did.physdesc: 1 item (121 leaves) : parchment ; 185 mm x 130 mm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Netherlands; Illuminated manuscript, in unidentified hand, on parchment, of a book of hours, use of Paris. Contains: calendar in Middle French (ff. 2r-13v), Gospel lessons (ff. 14r-19v), Obsecro te and O intemerata (ff. 20r-27r), Doulx dieu, doulx pere (ff. 28r-29v), Hours of the Virgin (ff. 30r-46v), Suffrages of St. Nicolas and St. Catherine (ff. 47-48r), (ff. 48v-63v), Penitential psalms (ff. 64r-74v), Litanies (ff. 74v-78v), Hours of the Cross (ff. 79r-80v), Hours of the Holy Spirit (81r-83v), Hours of the Virgin (ff. 84r-85v), Seven requests of our Lord in Middle French (ff. 86r-92v), Office for the dead and collection of prayers for the dead (ff. 93r-121r). Incomplete: several opening and closing folios missing. Layout: single column of 16 lines. Decoration: 11 full floral decorative pages. Several multi-line red and blue initials on a gold background; two-line gold initials on a blue and red background throughout. Rubricated throughout. Binding: 19th-century blind-tooled brown leather binding over pasteboard. Spine label: 6. Marbled paper pastedowns and endleaves.(Ege 6.1999; TR 12333/6)

otherfindaid.p: Also described in the Yale online catalog.

scopecontent.p: Description accompanying volume: Manuscript on 34 leaves of native glazed paper, each leaf measuring 10.75 X 7.5 inches, with six lines to the page. Interlinear and marginal notations in red. Full tan morocco with embossed floral design 857 A.H.

did.physdesc: Morocco. Manuscript. Written in Libian Charactors in black, red, yellow and blue. On 198 leaves of native paper, measuring 8.5 X 6 inches. 17 lines to the full page, within red and blue border. Full tan morocco, rebacked. With decorative Surahs at beginning and end of the book.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p:
Jazūlī, Muḥammad ibn Sulaymān, 1404-1465. Dalāʼil al-khayrāt Illuminated manuscript, rubricated. Flap binding. Date: H. Rajab 1261 / July 1845 Editor/Scribe: Hafiz al-Murtaza
An annotation dated H. 1278 / 1862 Osman Kamil Tarsusizade, the teacher of the Madrasa Mahmudiyya in Madina, records the chain of transmission of a poem that he learned from his teacher.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Item is inaccessible as of March 2024 pending preservation housing.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series consists of five complete portfolios of matted leaves: Fifteen Oriental Manuscript Leaves (2 sets), Fifty Original Leaves from Medieval Manuscripts, Original Leaves from Famous Bibles, and Leaves from Famous Bibles, Eight Centuries.
Many of the volumes from which the leaves originated are in Series III and IV. When known, Gwara's Handlist numbers are included in the description.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series consists of broken, incomplete, and fragments of manuscripts volumes. Leaves from many, potentially all, of the volumes were included on Ege's portfolios and inventory intended for sale. A few volumes appear complete but are in poor condition.
When known, Gwara's Ege handist numbers and Cleveland Museum of Art identifiers are included in the description.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The series is arranged by manuscript type and in rough chronological order therein.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series consists of intact and broken volumes, leaves, and fragments of printed books and one set of plates. Leaves of many of the volumes were included in Ege's portfolios and inventory intended for sale.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The series is arranged in two sections: Bibles and Other works. The bibles are arranged chronologically. Other works are arranged by author or title.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series primarily documents Otto F. Ege's career through writings, correspondence, photographs, newspaper clippings and a scrapbook. The correspondence filed under correspondence and in the scrapbook primarily consist of invitations and thank you notes addressed to Otto and Louise Ege. Ege's trade in manuscripts and rare books is documented in the catalogs, the Lima Public Library listings and worksheets. The notes and "Development of Sel[ection] of Fine leaves provide a glimpse into Ege's selection process. There are no records in the collection that document the financial side of Ege's career.
